title: 帷幕
summary:
Following a heart attack, Hercule Poirot informs Hastings that he will not see a doctor, despite Hastings' concerns and a visit from Curtis. Poirot declares that this will be his last case, noting that he is facing a cunning murderer referred to as "X" whose methods he cannot decipher. While Poirot's physical health is failing due to paralysis and heart issues, he maintains that his mind remains sharp. Hastings, however, privately doubts Poirot's mental acuity following the deaths of Mrs. Franklin and the near-death of Mrs. Littler.

The next day, Poirot agrees to see Dr. John Franklin, a researcher rather than a general practitioner. After examining Poirot, Franklin informs a worried Hastings that Poirot’s physical condition is "terrible" and that his time is running out. Franklin notes that Poirot is aware of his condition and is driven by a sense of responsibility to finish his work. The only available treatment is the use of nitroglycerin vials for potential attacks.

Franklin expresses a detached philosophy regarding death, stating that medicine's true purpose is improving human life rather than merely delaying the inevitable. He reveals that he has been hired for a position in Africa and will depart in ten days. Franklin also discusses his personal life, admitting to a lack of grief over the death of his wife, Barbara, describing himself as a selfish and stubborn man who moved on from their marriage quickly.
